bins.name: FERC Gas Jurisdiction Gap
bins.desc: APGA argues the ANOPR's jurisdictional scope is too narrow because large electric loads powered by interstate gas pipelines fall within FERC's regulatory reach but are not addressed. The association asks FERC to evaluate whether and how such facilities affect interstate commerce and to bring natural gas infrastructure into the large-load framework.
quotes: APGA supports the Secretary's emphasis on public engagement in shaping this rulemaking process. However, we are concerned that the current scope of the ANOPR overlooks the critical role natural gas infrastructure plays in supporting large electric loads.

As AI data centers and other high-demand facilities continue to grow, many are relying on natural gas-fired generation to meet their energy needs. Because many of these gas-fired facilities may draw from interstate pipelines regulated by FERC, their energy use has implications for interstate commerce.
APGA requests that as the Commission contemplate jurisdiction over large loads, they consider how these large loads may influence interstate natural gas markets and infrastructure.
APGA urges the Commission to incorporate natural gas considerations into this rulemaking to support an energy future that safeguards the interests of public gas utilities and their customers.

bins.name: Pipeline Capacity and Proximity
bins.desc: APGA highlights the proximate relationship between large electric loads and the gas transmission system: gas-fired data centers draw from the same interstate pipelines that public utilities depend on. Without coordination, pipeline capacity strain could impair public gas systems' ability to meet their obligation to serve, and firm transportation rights must be preserved.
quotes: they may impact the reliability of the natural gas transmission system, creating downstream impacts on APGA members' ability to reliably and affordably serve their communities and customers.
Any policy changes must preserve public gas utilities' flexibility in exercising their firm transportation rights and protect their ability to serve residential and commercial customers.
Without thoughtful planning and coordination, increased demand on pipelines could strain existing capacity and jeopardize the ability of public gas systems to meet their core obligation to serve.

bins.name: Expand Rulemaking to Gas Infrastructure
bins.desc: APGA's central ask is that FERC broaden the RM26-4 proceeding to cover natural gas infrastructure impacts from large loads. The association contends the current ANOPR addresses only the electric side while ignoring how AI data centers and similar facilities stress the interstate gas system that public utilities rely on.
quotes: APGA supports the Secretary's emphasis on public engagement in shaping this rulemaking process. However, we are concerned that the current scope of the ANOPR overlooks the critical role natural gas infrastructure plays in supporting large electric loads.

As AI data centers and other high-demand facilities continue to grow, many are relying on natural gas-fired generation to meet their energy needs. Because many of these gas-fired facilities may draw from interstate pipelines regulated by FERC, their energy use has implications for interstate commerce.
they may impact the reliability of the natural gas transmission system, creating downstream impacts on APGA members' ability to reliably and affordably serve their communities and customers.
APGA requests that as the Commission contemplate jurisdiction over large loads, they consider how these large loads may influence interstate natural gas markets and infrastructure.
APGA urges the Commission to incorporate natural gas considerations into this rulemaking to support an energy future that safeguards the interests of public gas utilities and their customers.

bins.name: Protect Firm Gas Transportation Rights
bins.desc: APGA asks that any new large-load policy preserve the firm transportation rights of public gas utilities and their flexibility to serve residential and commercial customers, warning that unchecked pipeline demand from large loads could jeopardize their core service obligations.
quotes: Any policy changes must preserve public gas utilities' flexibility in exercising their firm transportation rights and protect their ability to serve residential and commercial customers.
Without thoughtful planning and coordination, increased demand on pipelines could strain existing capacity and jeopardize the ability of public gas systems to meet their core obligation to serve.
